study_ref: Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk
summary: Introduced prospect theory: losses are weighted roughly twice as heavily as equivalent gains (loss aversion), and people evaluate outcomes relative to a reference point rather than in absolute terms.
Formalized mental accounting: people mentally sort money into separate, non-fungible accounts (rent, fun, savings) governed by different rules, even though money is objectively interchangeable.

study_ref: Gambling with the House Money and Trying to Break Even: The Effects of Prior Outcomes on Risky Choice
summary: Documented the house money effect: people take larger risks with money framed as recent winnings than with money framed as their own capital, even though the funds are identical.

researchers: Dan Ariely, George Loewenstein & Drazen Prelec
study_ref: 'Coherent Arbitrariness': Stable Demand Curves Without Stable Preferences
summary: Showed that arbitrary anchors (like the last two digits of a person's Social Security number) measurably shift how much people are willing to pay for a product, even though the anchor has no logical connection to its value.

study_ref: The Red and the Black: Mental Accounting of Savings and Debt
summary: Introduced the 'pain of paying' framework: paying with cash creates a more vivid, immediate sense of loss than paying with a credit card, decoupling the pain of payment from the pleasure of consumption.

study_ref: Always Leave Home Without It: A Further Investigation of the Credit-Card Effect on Willingness to Pay
summary: Found in a real auction that participants bidding with credit cards were willing to pay roughly twice as much as those bidding with cash for identical items — the 'credit-card premium.'

study_ref: Increasing Saving Behavior Through Age-Progressed Renderings of the Future Self
summary: People who interacted with an age-progressed digital rendering of themselves allocated more money to retirement savings, because it made their future self feel more psychologically real and continuous with their present self.

Used fMRI to show that activity in the nucleus accumbens (anticipated pleasure) versus the insula (anticipated pain) before seeing a price could predict, seconds later, whether a person would decide to buy a product.

researchers: Elizabeth Dunn, Lara Aknin & Michael Norton
study_ref: Spending Money on Others Promotes Happiness
summary: Spending money on other people ('prosocial spending') produced a more reliable boost in happiness than spending the same amount on oneself, across both survey data and a randomized experiment.

researchers: Kathleen Vohs, Nicole Mead & Miranda Goode
summary: Subtly reminding people of money (priming) made them behave more self-sufficiently and less communally — working harder alone, giving less help, preferring more personal space.

study_ref: The Disposition to Sell Winners Too Early and Ride Losers Too Long: Theory and Evidence
summary: Documented the disposition effect: investors are more willing to realize gains than losses, selling winning positions too early and holding losing positions too long.
